title: Happier Homemaker Farmhouse Table
summary: A double-leg farmhouse table where each corner is two 2x4s screwed face to face — extra mass for the same trip to the lumber yard, top run tight indoors or gapped for a porch.
description:
This table builds its legs in two passes rather than one: a first, shorter set of 2x4 legs gets joined by end stretchers into a unit, and then a SECOND set of full-length legs gets screwed to the outside of that unit. The result is a leg that reads as one thick post but is actually two 2x4s doing the work together — more mass and more screw-holding power than a single 2x4 leg, for the cost of doubling up stock you were already buying.

The 2x2 tabletop supports serve a specific, easy-to-underestimate purpose: they give you something to register the tabletop boards against as you lay them down, which is what lets five wide 2x8 boards land close and even instead of drifting apart board by board across a 6-foot span. Start screwing the top down from the middle and work outward in both directions — that keeps whatever small gap variance exists spread evenly across the width instead of compounding toward one edge.

Gap width is a genuine choice, not an oversight: tight for an indoor dining table, or a deliberate 1/4" gap between boards if this table lives somewhere wet, so rain has somewhere to go instead of pooling on the surface. Either finish works off the same cut list — only the assembly step's spacing changes.

tools.note: Optional for the apron-to-tabletop joints and building the top as one unit before attaching.
The source used a nailer for the leg joints where possible — screws work too, hidden on the insides.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Cut the longest boards first to conserve lumber.

materials.name: Exterior self-tapping screws, 2-1/2"
materials.note: Interior screws work fine for an indoor build.

materials.name: Wood glue
materials.note: Exterior-rated for outdoor use, interior-rated otherwise.

materials.name: Brad nails, 2"
materials.note: Galvanized if the table will live outdoors.

cutList.note: Two 2x4s screwed face to face per corner — four corners.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
Cut the longest 2x4 parts first, exactly as the material note says, or you will run out of usable board. From the six 8 ft 2x4s: two side aprons at 69", two end aprons at 30-1/4", four end stretchers at 24-1/4" and eight legs at 28-1/2". A 69" apron plus one 24-1/4" stretcher fills a board, and the legs pair up two or three to a board — all six boards get used.

From the 2x2, cut seven tabletop supports at 30-1/4", three to a board. The five 2x8 tabletop boards are 72" each, which is the full length of a 6 ft board, so use them as they come — pick your five straightest, flattest boards at the yard, because there is no length left to trim a bad end off.

steps.title: Build the first leg sets
steps.body:
Each end of the table uses two of the eight 28-1/2" legs and two of the four 24-1/4" end stretchers — one stretcher up near the top where the apron will land, and one lower down as a foot rail. Join a stretcher between each pair of legs so the legs finish 24-1/4" apart on the inside.

Glue every joint. A brad nailer with 2" brads is easy here because the heads hide in the joint line, but 2-1/2" screws driven from what becomes the inside face hold better and never show. Mark both ends off the same layout — the plan does not fix the lower stretcher's height, so the only thing that matters is that the two ends agree with each other.

steps.title: Add the first legs
steps.body:
Fasten each stretcher pair to its legs for real now, driving 2-1/2" screws from the inside faces with glue at every joint. Predrill with the drill-bit-set near the board ends so a 2x4 does not split on you.

Then stack one end assembly on the other and confirm every leg and stretcher lines up. Two ends that differ by even a little put a twist through the whole table that no amount of adjustment at the apron stage will take out.

steps.title: Double up the legs
steps.body:
Screw the remaining four 28-1/2" legs to the outside faces of the first four, face to face with glue between them, so each corner becomes a 3"-thick post built from two 2x4s. That is the whole trick of this table: the mass and screw-holding of a thick post out of stock you were already buying.

Line the outside edges and the bottom ends up flush before any screw goes in — a leg pair 1/8" out at the foot makes the finished table rock. Stagger the screws so they do not all land in one line down the glue joint, and drive them from the inside where they will not show.

steps.title: Attach the aprons
steps.body:
Set the two 69" side aprons and the two 30-1/4" end aprons across the tops of the leg assemblies so the four pieces form a closed frame tying all four corners — the side aprons are in the cut list but never get placed in the original instructions, and the tabletop needs them.

If you are attaching the top with pocket holes, drill 1-1/2" pocket holes facing upward on the inside faces of all four aprons now. Once the top is on there is no way to get a jig in there. Fasten with 2-1/2" screws and glue.

steps.title: Square the frame
steps.body:
Stand the frame up on a flat floor, measure both diagonals across the apron frame, and adjust until they match. Check it does not rock while you are there.

If the 69" side aprons run on the outside with the 30-1/4" end aprons between them, the frame comes out 69" by 33-1/4" — which under a 72" x 36-1/4" top gives an even 1-1/2" reveal on all four sides. That even reveal is the easiest confirmation that you have the apron pieces the right way round; if yours comes out flush at the ends instead, you have them the other way and should decide now whether you want an end overhang.

steps.title: Add the tabletop supports
steps.body:
Fasten the seven 30-1/4" 2x2 supports across the frame, under where the tabletop will sit, spaced evenly along the 69" side aprons — one at each end and five between leaves six bays of about 11-1/2". Mark them all out before driving a screw.

They are not just stiffeners. They give you a straight line to register each 2x8 against as you lay it, which is what keeps five wide boards landing close and even instead of drifting board by board across six feet.

If you have a pocket-hole jig, you can instead join the five top boards into one panel first and drop the finished top onto the aprons in a single operation.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body:
Lay the five 72" 2x8 boards face down on a flat surface with their edges together and set the frame on top. Five boards at 7-1/4" is 36-1/4" across — check that against your frame and centre it before a single screw goes in.

Starting with the middle board and working outward in both directions, drive 2-1/2" screws up through the 2x2 supports and the aprons into the boards. Working from the middle is what keeps whatever gap variance you have spread evenly instead of compounding toward one edge.

For indoor use, pull the boards tight together. For a table that gets rained on, hold a deliberate 1/4" gap between boards so water drains — that makes the top 37-1/4" wide instead of 36-1/4", so allow for it when you centre the frame. You can also drive a few screws down through the top into the end aprons in a deliberate pattern if you want the extra hold.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body:
Fill any visible fastener holes, sand every face by hand, and break the edges — a 2x8 top comes with mill glaze and sharp arrises, and neither takes finish well.

Finish to suit where the table lives: interior stain and topcoat indoors, or a genuine exterior finish if it gets rained on. If it goes outside, coat the end grain and the leg bottoms too — that is where water gets in first.

images.alt: Farmhouse-style dining table with doubled 2x4 legs and a wide plank top
